koderkushy's blog

Codeforces Round 1067 (Div. 2) Editorial
By koderkushy, history, 4 months ago,

2158A - Suspension Idea & Solution: kingmessi

Hint 1

Each red card immediately suspends a player. What is the best way to use rr red cards to maximize the number of distinct suspended players?

Hint 2

A player is suspended after receiving 2 yellow cards. With yy yellow cards, what is the maximum number of players you can suspend using only yellows?

Hint 3

If you add the maximum suspensions from red cards and yellow cards, can this total ever exceed nn? What should happen with extra cards in that case?

Tutorial

2158A - Suspension

To determine the maximum number of players suspended, we adopt a greedy approach. The goal is to distribute the available cards to trigger the suspension condition for as many distinct players as possible.

  • Red Cards: Since a single red card causes an immediate suspension, the most efficient use is to assign each red card to a unique player. Therefore, rr red cards can suspend exactly rr players.
  • Yellow Cards: A player requires 22 yellow cards to be suspended. To maximize the number of suspensions derived from yellow cards, we group them into pairs. The number of players that can be suspended using yellow cards is the integer division of yy by 2, denoted as ⌊y2⌋⌊y2⌋.

Combining these two, the total potential number of suspensions is r+⌊y2⌋r+⌊y2⌋. However, it is impossible to suspend more players than the total number of players nn present in the game. If the theoretical sum is greater than nn, the surplus cards can simply be distributed to players who are already being suspended (e.g., giving a yellow card to a player before they receive a red), effectively wasting the extra cards. Thus, the answer is constrained by nn. Hence, the final answer is min(n, r+⌊y2⌋)min(n, r+⌊y2⌋).

Time Complexity: O(1)O(1) per test case.

Solution
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

void solve(){
    int n,y,r;
    cin >> n;
    cin >> y >> r;
    cout << min(n,r+y/2) << "\n";
}

int main(){
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--){
        solve();
    }
}
Bonus

How do you calculate the minimum number of players suspended from the game possible?

2158B - Split Idea & Solution: kingmessi

Hint 1

Does the contribution of an element depend on its exact split (u,v)(u,v), or only on whether uu and vv are odd or even?

Hint 2

If every distinct element contributed its maximum possible amount, what would be the upper bound of the answer?

Hint 3

Elements with frequency of the form 4k+24k+2 can be split as (2k+1,2k+1)(2k+1,2k+1), creating 00 size imbalance. Elements with frequency of the form 4k4k can be split as (2k−1,2k+1)(2k−1,2k+1), creating 22 size imbalance.

Hint 4

The number of elements with odd total frequency is always even. How can these elements help correct any size imbalance?

Hint 5

In which situation does the size imbalance become impossible to fix, making the upper bound unreachable?

Hint 6

Consider the case when the number of distinct elements with frequency of the form 4k4k is odd. What happens if there exists at least one element with odd frequency? What happens if there are no elements with odd frequency?

Tutorial

2158B - Split

Given a subsequence of size 2n2n, we must split it into two subsequences pp and qq of size nn each. For any value with total frequency cntcnt, let uu and vv be its occurrences in pp and qq, respectively, with u+v=cntu+v=cnt. The contribution of this value is

(umod2)+(vmod2),(umod2)+(vmod2),
so it can be 00, 11, or 22.

Classify the distinct values as follows:

x=#{cnt odd},y=#{cnt≡2 (mod 4)},z=#{cnt≡0 (mod 4)}.x=#{cnt odd},y=#{cnt≡2 (mod 4)},z=#{cnt≡0 (mod 4)}.

The theoretical maximum possible score is

x+2y+2z.x+2y+2z.

For values with odd cntcnt, exactly one of uu or vv must be odd, so the contribution is always 11. For cnt=4k+2cnt=4k+2, choosing u=v=2k+1u=v=2k+1 gives contribution 22 and keeps the sizes balanced. For cnt=4kcnt=4k, one of the splits with contribution 22 is (u,v)=(2k+1,2k−1)(u,v)=(2k+1,2k−1), which creates a size imbalance of ±2±2.

If zz is even, these imbalances cancel. If x>0x>0, we can fix any remaining imbalance using one odd-frequency value — without affecting the score. Thus, whenever x>0x>0 or zz is even, the theoretical maximum is achievable:

x+2y+2z.x+2y+2z.

Now consider the case x=0x=0 and zz odd. All frequencies are even. To get contribution 22 from a value with cnt=4k+2cnt=4k+2, the odd split gives size difference 0(mod4)0(mod4). For cnt=4kcnt=4k, any odd split gives size difference 2(mod4)2(mod4). Summing these over all values:

∑i(ui−vi)≡2z(mod4).∑i(ui−vi)≡2z(mod4).
Since zz is odd, this is congruent to 22, contradicting |p|=|q||p|=|q|. Thus one value must contribute 00 instead of 22, reducing the total by 22.

Therefore, when x=0x=0 and zz odd, the maximum score is

x+2y+2z−2.x+2y+2z−2.

Hence the final answer is

{x+2y+2z,x+2y+2z−2,if x>0 or z is even,if x=0 and z is odd.{x+2y+2z,if x>0 or z is even,x+2y+2z−2,if x=0 and z is odd.

Time complexity is O(n)O(n) per test case.

Solution
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

void solve(){
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<int> a(2*n);
    for(int i = 0;i < 2*n;i++){
        cin >> a[i];
    }

    vector<int> cnt(2*n+1);
    for(auto &x : a)cnt[x]++;

    int x = 0,y = 0,z = 0;
    for(int i = 1;i <= 2*n;i++){
        if(cnt[i] == 0)continue; 
        if(cnt[i]&1)x++;         //odd elements
        else if(cnt[i]%4)y++;    //element of form 4*k + 2
        else z++;                //element of form 4*k 
    }

    int ans = x+2*y+2*z;
    if((z&1) && x == 0)ans -= 2; 
    cout << ans << "\n";
}

int main(){
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--){
        solve();
    }
}

2158C - Annoying Game Idea & Solution: kingmessi

Hint 1

If Alice makes a move and Bob immediately performs the opposite move on the same index (and vice versa), what happens to the array?

Hint 2

How does the parity of kk affect who makes the last move and therefore who can enforce the final uncancelled change?

Hint 3

Using the cancellation idea, can the entire game be reduced to a simpler one with exactly kmod2kmod2 effective moves?

Hint 4

If Alice changes aiai, only subarrays containing ii can improve. What are the maximum-sum subarrays that end at ii and start at ii, and how can combining them give the best subarray passing through ii?

Tutorial

2158C - Annoying Game

Let S(a)S(a) denote the maximum non-empty subarray sum of array aa. We determine the final score by analyzing the game based on the parity of kk, assuming optimal play from both Alice and Bob.

First, consider the case where kk is even. Bob has a strategy to ensure the final score is at most the initial S(a)S(a). Since Bob plays the even-numbered turns, he can adopt a cancellation strategy. Immediately after Alice modifies an index ii by adding bibi, Bob can modify the same index ii by subtracting bibi. This ensures that after every pair of turns, the array is restored to the state it was in before Alice's turn. After kk turns, the array is identical to the initial array. Thus, Bob forces the result ≤S(ainitial)≤S(ainitial).

We must verify if Bob can force the score lower, or if Alice can guarantee at least S(ainitial)S(ainitial). Alice adopts the following strategy: Let the subarray [l,r][l,r] be the subarray yielding the initial maximum sum. Alice identifies an index pp such that l≤p≤rl≤p≤r and bpbp is the maximum value among all bb in that range. In her first turn, Alice adds bpbp to apap. For all subsequent turns, Alice adopts the cancellation strategy against Bob: if Bob modifies index xx, Alice immediately modifies index xx in her next turn to restore it.

This strategy ensures that just before Bob's very last move (turn kk), the array has exactly one modification: apap is increased by bpbp. Bob must now make a move to minimize the score. To reduce the sum of the subarray [l,r][l,r], Bob must choose an index qq within the range [l,r][l,r] and subtract bqbq. The new sum becomes S(ainitial)+bp−bqS(ainitial)+bp−bq. Since Alice chose pp such that bpbp is maximal in that range, bp≥bqbp≥bq. Consequently, S(ainitial)+bp−bq≥S(ainitial)S(ainitial)+bp−bq≥S(ainitial). Bob is forced to choose q=pq=p to simply undo Alice's first move; otherwise, the score remains strictly greater than S(ainitial)S(ainitial). Thus, for even kk, the final score is exactly S(ainitial)S(ainitial).

Next, consider the case where kk is odd. Alice has a strategy to maximize the result of a single independent move. She performs her desired modification on turn 1. For any subsequent move by Bob, Alice uses her next turn to cancel it. Since Alice makes the last move (turn kk), she ensures all of Bob's actions are neutralized. Conversely, Bob can prevent Alice from achieving anything more than a single move by canceling Alice's actions during the first k−1k−1 turns. The game reduces to Alice making exactly one move ai←ai+biai←ai+bi to maximize S(a)S(a).

To solve this efficiently, we use prefix and suffix maximums. Let L[i]L[i] be the maximum sum of a non-empty subarray ending at ii, and R[i]R[i] be the maximum sum of a non-empty subarray starting at ii. If Alice modifies index ii, the maximum sum passing through ii is the sum of the maximum prefix ending at ii and the maximum suffix starting at ii, adjusted for modified value. Since both L[i]L[i] and R[i]R[i] include the original aiai, we subtract aiai once to avoid double counting, then add bibi (denoting Alice modification). The final answer is the maximum of the initial score and the best possible modified sum:

max1≤i≤n(L[i]+R[i]−ai+bi)max1≤i≤n(L[i]+R[i]−ai+bi)

Solution
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

void solve(){
    int n,k;
    cin >> n >> k;
    vector<int> a(n),b(n);
    for(int i = 0;i < n;i++)cin >> a[i];
    for(int i = 0;i < n;i++)cin >> b[i];
    k &= 1; //It can be reduced as 0/1 move game

    vector<long long> L(n); //maximum sum non empty subarray ending at each position
    for(int i = 0;i < n;i++){
        L[i] = (i && L[i-1]>0 ? L[i-1] : 0ll) + a[i]; 
    }   

    vector<long long> R(n); //maximum sum non empty subarray starting at each position
    for(int i = n-1;i >= 0;i--){
        R[i] = (i+1<n && R[i+1]>0 ? R[i+1] : 0ll) + a[i];
    }

    if(k == 0){
        long long ans = *max_element(L.begin(),L.end());
        cout << ans << "\n";
    }
    else{
        long long ans = LONG_MIN;
        for(int i = 0;i < n;i++){
            ans = max(ans,L[i]+R[i]-a[i]+b[i]);
        }
        cout << ans << "\n";
    }
}

int main(){
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--){
        solve();
    }
}
Bonus

How do you calculate the final score if goals of Alice and Bob were swapped?

2158D - Palindrome Flipping Idea & Solution: kingmessi and abc864197532

Hint 1

Instead of directly transforming ss into tt, is it enough to know how to transform any string into an intermediate string?

Hint 2

If you know how to turn any string into 0n0n in at most nn operations, how can you use that to transform ss into tt in at most 2n2n operations?

Hint 3

If there is at least one position pp with sp=sp+1sp=sp+1. Can you grow a block of equal bits starting from [p,p+1][p,p+1]?

Hint 4

How do you ensure such a position pp in strictly alternating string after exactly one operation?

Hint 5

When trying to grow the block to the right (or left), what happens if the next bit is equal to the block's boundary bit?
What if it is different? Can you use flipping a palindromic block (all equal bits) to force the boundary to match the next bit?

Tutorial

2158D - Palindrome Flipping

We use 11-based indexing and assume n≥4n≥4. An allowed operation chooses 1≤l<r≤n1≤l<r≤n such that sl,rsl,r is a palindrome, and flips all its bits. To transform ss into tt, it is enough to show that any string can be converted to 0n0n using at most nn operations. Indeed, if we find a sequence of operations turning ss into 0n0n and a (possibly different) sequence turning tt into 0n0n, then by performing the second sequence in reverse order starting from 0n0n, we obtain tt. Thus the total number of operations needed to convert ss into tt is at most twice the number needed to convert a single string into 0n0n.

We therefore focus on reducing an arbitrary string ss to 0n0n using at most nn operations. If ss is alternating, then since n≥4n≥4, the substring s2,4s2,4 is either 010010 or 101101, hence a palindrome. Flipping s2,4s2,4 changes s2s2 while leaving s1s1 unchanged, so afterwards s1=s2s1=s2. Thus in at most one step the string becomes non-alternating.

Now assume ss is non-alternating. Let pp be the smallest index with sp=sp+1sp=sp+1, and initialize a block of equal bits as L=pL=p, R=p+1R=p+1. We now expand this block until it covers the entire string.

Consider the right boundary. If R<nR<n, then:

1. If sR+1=sRsR+1=sR, we simply extend the block by setting R:=R+1R:=R+1.

2. If sR+1≠sRsR+1≠sR, then sL,RsL,R is a palindrome (all bits in the block are equal). Flipping sL,RsL,R toggles every bit in the block but keeps them equal, and also toggles sRsR while leaving sR+1sR+1 unchanged. Thus afterwards sR=sR+1sR=sR+1, and the block expands to R:=R+1R:=R+1.

A symmetric step expands the block to the left whenever L>1L>1. Each operation increases the size of the block by exactly 11. After at most n−1n−1 such expansions, the block covers all positions, so the string is constant.

If the resulting string is 0n0n, we are done. If it is 1n1n, then s1,ns1,n is itself a palindrome, and flipping it once produces 0n0n. Thus at most nn operations always suffice to reach 0n0n.

Solution
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

vector<array<int,2>> reduce(string &s){
    int n = s.size();
    vector<array<int,2>> op;

    int p = -1; // position p such that two consecutive characters are same
    for(int i = 0;i < n-1;i++){
        if(s[i] == s[i+1])p = i;
    }
    if(p == -1){ // string is alternating
        op.push_back({0,2}); // substring of first three character must be palindrome
        s[0] ^= 1;s[1] ^= 1;
        p = 2;
    }

    char value = s[p+1];
    int L = p,R = p+1; // We maintain [L,R] such that all position in it have the same value

    while(R+1 < n){
        if(s[R+1] != value)op.push_back({L,R});
        R++;value = s[R];
    }

    while(L){
        if(s[L-1] != value)op.push_back({L,R});
        L--;value = s[L];
    }

    if(value == '1')op.push_back({0,n-1}); // We want to make all zeroes

    return op;
}

void solve(){
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    string s,t;
    cin >> s >> t;

    auto operation_s = reduce(s); // operations to reduce s to all zeroes
    auto operation_t = reduce(t); // operations to reduce t to all zeroes

    reverse(operation_t.begin(),operation_t.end());

    int moves = operation_s.size() + operation_t.size();
    cout << moves << "\n";
    for(auto &[x,y] : operation_s)cout << x+1 << " " << y+1 << "\n";
    for(auto &[x,y] : operation_t)cout << x+1 << " " << y+1 << "\n";

}

int main(){
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--){
        solve();
    }
}

2158E - Sink Idea & Solution: kingmessi

Hint 1

How would you solve the problem if every value in the grid was distinct?

Hint 2

When some cells have the same value, is it natural to treat a connected component of equal valued cells as a single group?
For such a group, how can you decide whether this entire group needs a hole?

Hint 3

After a single query (decreasing the value of one cell), can the beauty increase by more than 1?
How many groups from previous grid can stop needing a hole because they now have a strictly smaller neighbour?

Hint 4

How can you model the evolution of the grid as a graph, where edges reflect either adjacency in the grid or "time adjacency" between different versions of the same cell?

Hint 5

If a cell receives kk decreasing updates over all queries, can you model it as a chain of k+1k+1 nodes (one per value over time)?
How does connecting each new node to the latest versions of its neighbours help you maintain connected components of equal values efficiently?

Tutorial

2158E - Sink

We maintain the grid using a graphical representation of each cell. Initially, for every cell (i,j)(i,j) we create one node storing its initial value ai,jai,j. Later, whenever a query decreases the value of a cell, we create a new node representing the updated value of that cell. Thus each cell is represented by a sequence of nodes created over time.

For every newly created node, we define its neighbours as follows. It has up to four neighbours corresponding to the latest created nodes of its grid-adjacent cells, and it also has a neighbour equal to the immediately previous node created for the same cell (if such a node exists). Therefore each node has at most five neighbours.

We define a group to be the connected component in this node-graph consisting of nodes that all have the same value, using adjacency defined exactly as above. That is, a group is a maximal set of nodes of equal value where every node is reachable from every other using only edges between equal-valued neighbours. We maintain these groups using a disjoint-set union structure over all created nodes.

A group of value vv is declared to be important if no node in this group has any neighbour with strictly smaller value than vv. If a group is not a sink, then it is covered by some neighbour of smaller value and does not require a hole. The beauty of the grid is therefore the number of important groups.

Since queries only decrease values, the behaviour of the structure is very local. When a query decreases the value of cell (i,j)(i,j) to v′v′, we create a new node storing v′v′ and connect it to its at most five neighbours as described earlier. This new node may merge with some of its equal-valued neighbours, forming the new group that contains the updated cell.

Only the groups touching this new node can possibly change their status as important. There are two kinds of potential changes:

1. The new group containing the updated node might change from being non-important to an important group if, after the update, it no longer has any strictly smaller neighbour.

2. Any neighbouring group, whose value is now strictly larger than v′v′, may change from important to non-important because the updated node becomes a strictly smaller neighbour.

These checks are straightforward to perform because they involve only the few neighbours of the newly created node. Thus each update requires only constant work apart from the DSU merges.

After creating the initial nmnm nodes, and after processing each query, we count the groups that are important under the current node graph. This count is exactly the beauty of the grid at that moment.

Time complexity is O((nm+q)⋅α(nm+q))O((nm+q)⋅α(nm+q)), where αα is the inverse-Ackermann function.

Solution
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
const int N = 400005;
int parent[N],siz[N],important[N],val[N];
 
void make_set(int v,int x) {
    parent[v] = v;siz[v] = 1;important[v] = 1;val[v] = x;
}
 
int find_set(int v) {
    if (v == parent[v])return v;
    return parent[v] = find_set(parent[v]);
}
 
void union_sets(int a, int b) {
    a = find_set(a);
    b = find_set(b);
    if (a != b) {
        if (siz[a] < siz[b])swap(a, b);
        parent[b] = a;
        siz[a] += siz[b];
        important[a] = min(important[a],important[b]);
    }
}
 
int n,m;
 
vector<array<int,2>> neighbours(int r,int c){
    vector<array<int,2>> v;
    if(r)v.push_back({r-1,c});
    if(r+1 < n)v.push_back({r+1,c});
    if(c)v.push_back({r,c-1});
    if(c+1 < m)v.push_back({r,c+1});
    return v;
}
 
void solve()
{
    cin >> n >> m;
    vector<vector<int>> a(n,vector<int>(m));  // latest value of a given cell
    vector<vector<int>> ls(n,vector<int>(m)); // latest node created for a given cell
    for(int i = 0;i < n;i++){
        for(int j = 0;j < m;j++){
            cin >> a[i][j];
            ls[i][j] = m*i+j;
        }
    }
 
    for(int i = 0;i < n;i++){
        for(int j = 0;j < m;j++){
            make_set(m*i+j,a[i][j]);
            for(auto &[p,q] : neighbours(i,j)){
                if(a[p][q] < a[i][j])important[m*i+j] = 0;
            }      
        }
    }
 
    for(int i = 0;i < n;i++){
        for(int j = 0;j < m;j++){
            for(auto &[p,q] : neighbours(i,j)){
                if(a[p][q] == a[i][j])union_sets(m*i+j,m*p+q);
            }
        }
    }
 
    int ans = 0;
    for(int i = 0;i < n;i++){
        for(int j = 0;j < m;j++){
            if(parent[m*i+j] == m*i+j)ans+=important[m*i+j];
        }
    }
    
    cout << ans << "\n";
 
    int q;
    cin >> q;
    int nd = n*m;
 
    for(int i = 0;i < q;i++){
        int r,c,x;
        cin >> r >> c >> x;
        r--;c--;
        x = a[r][c] - x;
        set<int> s;
 
        int mn = INT_MAX;
        for(auto &[p,q] : neighbours(r,c)){
            s.insert(find_set(ls[p][q]));
            mn = min(mn,a[p][q]);
        }
        s.insert(find_set(ls[r][c]));
 
        for(auto &y : s){
            ans -= important[y];
        }
 
        for(auto &y : s){
            if(val[y] > x)important[y] = 0;
        }
 
        make_set(nd,x);
        if(mn < x)important[nd] = 0;
        else important[nd] = 1;
 
 
        for(auto &y : s){
            if(val[y] == x){
                union_sets(y,nd);
            }
        }
 
        a[r][c] = x;
        ls[r][c] = nd++;
 
        s.clear();
        for(auto &[p,q] : neighbours(r,c)){
            s.insert(find_set(ls[p][q]));
        }
        s.insert(find_set(ls[r][c]));
 
        for(auto &y : s){
            ans += important[y];
        }
 
        cout << ans << "\n";
    }
 
}
 
signed main(){
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--)
        solve();
    return 0;
}

2158F2 - Distinct GCDs (Hard Version) Idea & Solution: koderkushy

Tutorial

2158F2 - Distinct GCDs (Hard Version)

Say we are using kk distinct elements denoted by X={x1,x2,…,xk}X={x1,x2,…,xk}. What is the maximum length of an integer sequence aa such that ∀i∀i ai∈Xai∈X and GCDs of pairwise adjacent elements of aa are all distinct?

Construct an undirected complete graph with XX as the set of vertices and label the edge between xixi and xjxj with gcd(xi,xj)gcd(xi,xj) for all valid i,ji,j (loops with i=ji=j too). If all the edge labels are distinct, an upper bound to the above question is the length of the longest path in this graph that visits any edge at most once.

For odd kk, it is possible to visit all k(k+1)2k(k+1)2 edges and such a path is called an Eulerian tour. No graph with other than zero or two odd-degree vertices can have an Euler tour as only the first and last nodes of the path may have odd degrees (and none if they coincide). Thus, for even kk we need to flip the degree parity of at least k−2k−2 vertices. The optimal way to do that is by deleting a matching of size k−22k−22. Thus the maximum length of such a path is

k(k+1)2−k−22=k22+1k(k+1)2−k−22=k22+1

Constructing an Eulerian tour has a simple solution described here

Now, it remains to find XX such that the edge labels are all distinct, i.e., gcd(xi,xj)gcd(xi,xj) are all pairwise distinct for all unordered pairs (i,j)(i,j) and xi≤1018xi≤1018. For the easy version of the problem, xi=2i−1⋅3k−ixi=2i−1⋅3k−i will suffice. For the hard version, we can improve by extending the above to higher dimensions as

xi+j⋅M=2i+j⋅3i⋅5M−1−i⋅7j⋅11M−1−j ∀0≤i,j<Mxi+j⋅M=2i+j⋅3i⋅5M−1−i⋅7j⋅11M−1−j ∀0≤i,j<M

Set M=10M=10 to get |X|=100|X|=100 that allows us to construct aa with over 50005000 integers.

Solution (Python)
from copy import deepcopy

def get_nums(k: int=10):
    nums = [
        2**(i + j) * 3**i * 5**j * 7**(k-1-i) * 11**(k-1-j)
        for i in range(k) for j in range(k)
    ]

    return nums

def eulerian_paths(max_k: int = 100):
    paths = [[], [0, 0], [1,1,0,0]]

    for k in range(3, max_k + 1):
        path = deepcopy(paths[k - 2])
        x, y = k-2, k-1

        if k % 2:
            path.extend([x, x, y, y])
            for j in range(1, x, 2):
                path.extend([j, x, j+1, y])
            path.append(0)
        else:
            path.extend([x, x, 1, y, y])
            for j in range(2, x, 2):
                path.extend([j, x, j+1, y])
            path.append(0)

        paths.append(path)

    return paths

def path_len(k: int):
    return 1 + (k*k+k)//2 - (0 if k%2 else k//2-1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    nums = get_nums(10)
    paths = eulerian_paths(100)

    for i in range(1, 101):
        assert len(paths[i]) == path_len(i)

    t = int(input())

    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(input())

        l, r = 0, 100
        while l < r-1:
            m = r - (r-l)//2
            if path_len(m) < n:
                l = m
            else:
                r = m

        path = paths[r]
        assert len(path) >= n

        a = [nums[i] for i in path[: n]]
        print(*a)
Solution (C++)
#include "bits/stdc++.h"
using namespace std;


constexpr int64_t poww (int64_t a, int64_t p) {
    assert(p >= 0);
    int64_t res = 1;
    while (p > 0) {
        if (p & 1) res *= a;
        a *= a, p >>= 1;
    }
    return res;
}

constexpr int32_t path_len(const int32_t n) {
	return 1 + (n*n + n) / 2 - (n % 2 ? 0: n/2 - 1);
}

vector nums(0, 0ll);
vector walks(0, vector(0, 0));

void load_nums(const size_t p=10) {
	nums.resize(p * p);

	for (int i = 0; i < p; ++i)
		for (int j = 0; j < p; ++j)
			nums[i * 10 + j] = poww(2,(i+j)) * poww(3,i) * poww(5,j) * poww(7,(9-i)) * poww(11,(9-j));
}

void load_walks(const size_t max_k=100) {
	walks.resize(max_k + 1);
	walks[1] = vector({0, 0});
	walks[2] = vector({1, 1, 0, 0});

	for (int k = 3; k <= max_k; k++) {
		auto& walk = walks[k];
		walk = walks[k - 2];
		const int x = k-2, y = k-1;

		if (k % 2) {
			walk.push_back(x);
			walk.push_back(x);
			walk.push_back(y);
			walk.push_back(y);
			for (int j = 1; j < x; j += 2)
				walk.push_back(j),
				walk.push_back(x),
				walk.push_back(j+1),
				walk.push_back(y);
			walk.push_back(0);
		} else {
			walk.push_back(x);
			walk.push_back(x);
			walk.push_back(1);
			walk.push_back(y);
			walk.push_back(y);
			for (int j = 2; j < x; j += 2)
				walk.push_back(j),
				walk.push_back(x),
				walk.push_back(j+1),
				walk.push_back(y);
			walk.push_back(0);
		}
	}
}

void solve (const int kase) {

    int n; cin >> n;

    int l = 0, r = 100;
    while (l < r - 1) {
    	int m = r - (r-l) / 2;
    	if (path_len(m) < n)
    		l = m;
    	else
    		r = m;
    }

    vector a(n, 0ll);
    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
    	a[i] = nums[walks[r][i]];
    	cout << a[i] << ' ' ;
    }

    vector gcds(n-1, 0ll);
    for (size_t i = 1; i < n; ++i) {
    	gcds[i-1] = gcd(a[i], a[i - 1]);
    }

    assert(set(gcds.begin(), gcds.end()).size() == n-1);

    cout << '\n';
}

int main () {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0), cin.tie(0);

    load_nums();
    load_walks();

    for (size_t i = 1 ; i < 101; ++i)
    	assert(walks[i].size() == path_len(i));

    int t; cin >> t, assert(t >= 0);
    for (int i = 0; t--; )
        solve(++i);

}

vinith_katariya
4 months ago, show (+8) #

vinith_katariya
4 months ago, hide # |
 
+3

For anyone who needs just an add-on to understand the editorial for B a little better

I really got cooked by this problem but finally came up with a solution that happened to match the editorial 2 hrs post contest lol

Spoiler

-> Split (my brain into 2 ;-;)

odd freq numbers will always add exactly one to our ans, so treat them as fillers to make sequence of size 'n'

For even frequency numbers there are two cases

let f be such a freq (f is even) Case: 1 f / 2 is odd then in this case we can safely put f / 2 into each sequence and add 2 to the ans.

Case 2: f / 2 is even (i.e f is a multiple of 4) then the split with the minimal difference we can do is f / 2 — 1, f / 2 + 1 for eg if f = 8, f / 2 = 4, but if we put f / 2 occurences in each seq that wont add anything to our score

Instead if we put 3 , 5, then we can still add 2 to our ans.

But this will create a size diff of 2 in p & q. Suppose we put 3 in p and 5 in q

So to fix this, the next time we get f / 2 as even, we can put the extra 2 elements to p to balance it out again.

So it means, if we have an EVEN number of frequencies f such that f is a multiple of 4, then we can still always split all even freq and add 2 to the ans.

But if we have an odd number of such frequencies, then if we have atleast some odd frequencies to act as filler we are still good otherwise, exactly ONE of the even freq divisble by 4 will not contribute to the score at all and we dont add that.

      Code
      #include <bits/stdc++.h>

using i64 = long long;
using u64 = unsigned long long;

#define endl '\n'

void solve() {
  int n;
  std::cin >> n;

  std::vector<int> a(2 * n + 1);

  for (int i = 0; i < 2 * n; i++) {
    int x;
    std::cin >> x;
    a[x]++;
  }

  int ans = 0;
  int odd = 0;
  int even = 0;
  int mul4 = 0;

  for (int i = 0; i <= 2 * n; i++) {
    if (a[i]) {
      if (a[i] & 1) {
        odd++;
      } else {
        even++;
      }

      if (a[i] % 4 == 0) {
        mul4++;
      }
    }
  }

  if (mul4 & 1) {
    if (odd) {
      ans = odd + even * 2;
    } else {
      ans = odd + (even - 1) * 2;
    }
  } else {
    ans = odd + even * 2;
  }

  std::cout << ans << endl;
}

int main() {

  std::ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
  std::cin.tie(nullptr);

  int t;
  std::cin >> t;
  while (t--) {
    solve();
  }

  return 0;
}

In C after the idea of differentiating between kk odd and even you can also just use Kadane's Algorithm: Maximum Subarray Problem.

See here 351210965. With even kk we just use Kadane's algorithm. With odd kk we add another state: The best possible sum, assuming we already used Alices one move to add bibi to one number.

      arin_chandurkar
      3 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      you could treat k even with Kadane and k odd with 2 cases-

      1) If every element is less than 0, then the answer is simply max(a[i]+b[i]) over all is

      2) else you can write a dp[i][0]-> b already chosen, dp[i][1] -> b not chosen and then

      dp[i][1]=max(max(dp[i-1][1]+a[i],0),max(dp[i-1][0]+a[i]+b[i],0)); dp[i][0]=max(dp[i-1][0]+a[i],0);

      inspire purely by Kadane's algorithm

  • »

    zapwiz22
    4 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    zapwiz22
    4 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    You are assuming that the answer for the 'odd k' cases will lie in the subarray which includes the subarray found using kadane's algorithm, but this is not necessarily true. Below is one of the cases when this would fail.

    1
4 5 
3 -5 2 0
3 2 4 5

    The answer that your solution would give is 6 (includes the 0th index only) while the actual solution is 7 (the subarray from 2nd to 3rd index along with b[3]).

    → Reply

In Problem F2, I wrote a randomization program and found 100 numbers with pairwise distinct gcd values.

randomization program

The probability of this randomization program succeeding was low, but I was lucky enough to stumble upon one such set of numbers.

After obtaining these 100 numbers, the construction method for this problem is the same as in CF1981D.

Can anyone tell me the problem with my code for D?:

Code
// code by phongln
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define ll long long
#define fi first
#define se second
#define fo(i, a, b, k) for(ll i=a; i<=b; i+=k)
#define fod(i, a, b, k) for(ll i=a; i>=b; i-=k)
#define lop(x, s) for(auto x : s)
#define sz(s) s.size()
#define all(s) s.begin(), s.end()
#define pll pair<ll, ll>
#define vll vector<ll>
#define pb push_back
#define mp make_pair
#define sq(a) (a)*(a)
#define cb(a) (a)*(a)*(a)
#define NAME "TEST"
const ll inf=1e18;
const ll mod=1e9+7;
const ll maxn=1e6+5;
int dx[] = {-1, 1, 0, 0}, dy[] = {0, 0, -1, 1};

struct mo{
	ll t, r;
	bool f;
};

ll n;
string s, t;
vector<pll> res;

bool ck(string &s, ll l, ll r){
	ll len=r-l+1;
	fo(k, 0, len/2-1, 1){
		if(s[l+k]!=s[r-k]) return 0;
	}
	return 1;
}

void op(string &s, vector<pll> &res, ll l, ll r){
	res.pb(mp(l+1, r+1));
	fo(k, l, r, 1){
		if(s[k]=='0') s[k]='1';
		else s[k]='0';
	}
}

void init(){
}

void solve(){
	res.clear();
	cin >> n >> s >> t;
	fo(i, 0, n-2, 1){
		if(s[i]==t[i]) continue;
		vector<mo> can;
		fo(r, i+1, n-1, 1){
			if(ck(s, i, r)){
				bool a=(r==n-1);
				can.pb({1, r, a});
			}
		}
		if(i<n-2){
			fo(r, i+2, n-1, 1){
				if(ck(s, i+1, r)){
					bool a=(r==n-1);
					can.pb({2, r, a});
				}
			}
		}
		if(sz(can)==0){
			cout << "-1\n";
			return;
		}
		ll best=-1;
		bool cm=(s[n-1]==t[n-1]);
		fo(k, 0, sz(can)-1, 1){
			bool w=cm^can[k].f;
			if(w){
				best=k;
				break;
			}
		}
		if(best==-1) best=0;
		mo m=can[best];
		if(m.t==1){
			op(s, res, i, m.r);
		}
		else{
			op(s, res, i+1, m.r);
			op(s, res, i, i+1);
		}
	}
	if(s!=t){
		cout << "-1\n";
		return;
	}
	else{
		cout << sz(res) << '\n';
		lop(p, res){
			cout << p.fi << ' ' << p.se << '\n';
		}
	}
}

int main()
{
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(NULL);
    // freopen(NAME".INP", "r", stdin);
    // freopen(NAME".OUT", "w", stdout);
    init();
    ll t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--){
        solve();
    }
    return 0;
}

Problem D can also solve through bfs .

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    gevak
    4 months ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    gevak
    4 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +1

    Solution from editorial looks complicated. Alternate solutions are easier to come up to, though probably a bit harder to implement.
    You already mentioned the BFS, I want to add that brute force is also possible.
    So, we move from left to right, transforming current element of s into t, until 4 elements are left (4 is a guess, coming from fact that n is >= 4 by problem statement).
    It can be done greedily. Say, current element of s is 1 and in t it's 0. Let's find next closest 1 in s. Interval between current element and that next 1 will be palindrome (because there are 1's on sides and all 0's in the middle). If there are no more 1's in s, it means all elements to the right are 0's, so flip them all since they form a palindrome, now we have all 1's, so flip again to get all 0's.
    Thus, in at most 2 * (n — 4) operations we transform first n — 4 elements.
    For the remaining 4 elements use either bruteforce of BFS. There are only 2^4 * 2^4 combinations, so we can just try all of them and make sure that all can be done withing 8 operations.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      NV_09
      4 months ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      NV_09
      4 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      how did you calculated that " There are only 2^4 * 2^4 combinations ".

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        gevak
        4 months ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        gevak
        4 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        There are 2 ^ 4 binary strings with 4 elements, because each element has 2 possible values (0 and 1). We have 2 binary strings containing 4 elements each, thus for each of 2^4 possible values of 1st string, there 2^4 combinations of the second string. Or we can view 2 strings of lenght 4 as a 1 string of length for. Thus we have 2^8, which is equal to 2^4 * 2^4.

        → Reply
»

Did any one write recursive dp solution to problem C?

Here is a version

Spoiler


void solve()
{
    int n,k;
    cin>>n>>k;
    k%=2;
    int a[n] , b[n];
    fl(i,n)cin>>a[i];
    fl(i,n)cin>>b[i];

    const int INF = -4e18;

    int dp[n][3][2];
    bool vis[n][3][2]; 
    memset(vis, 0, sizeof(vis));


    function<int(int,int,int)>recur = [&](int index,int start,int t)->int{
        if(index==n){
            if(start==2)return 0;
            return INF;
        }
        int &ans = dp[index][start][t];
        if(vis[index][start][t])return ans;
       
        vis[index][start][t] = 1; 
        ans = INF;
        

        if(start==0){
            ans = max(ans,recur(index+1,start,t));
            // start with taking contribution
            if(t>=1){
                ans = max(ans, a[index] + b[index] + recur(index+1,1,t-1));
                // 1-sized array case
                ans = max(ans, a[index] + b[index] + recur(index+1,2,t-1));
            }
            // start without contribution
            ans = max(ans, a[index] + recur(index+1,1,t));
            // 1-sized array case
            ans = max(ans, a[index] + recur(index+1,2,t));
        }else if(start==1){
            // end or continue
            ans = max(ans,a[index] +recur(index+1,2,t));
            ans = max(ans,a[index] + recur(index+1,start,t));
            // end or continue with use
            if(t>=1){
                ans = max(ans,a[index]+b[index] + recur(index+1,start,t-1));
                ans = max(ans,a[index]+b[index] + recur(index+1,2,t-1));
            }
        }else{
            // just move
            ans = max(ans,recur(index+1,start,t));
        }
        return ans;
    };

    println(recur(0,0,k));
}

+3

D: Repeat: flip the substring from the first 1 to the next 1.

When there is only one 1 left, it can be zeroed out in at most 3 flips.

0

simpler method to get "11..."

// this is code
vector<pair<ll,ll>> cal(string s){
    vector<pair<ll,ll>> p;
    if(s[0]!=s[1]){
        if(s[1]==s[2]){
            s[1]=s[2]=s[0];
            p.push_back({1,2});
        }
        else{
            if(s[2]==s[3]){
                p.push_back({2,3});
                p.push_back({1,3});
                s[1]=s[2];
            }else{
                p.push_back({1,3});
                s[2]=s[1];
                s[1]=s[3]=s[0];
            }
        }
    }
    for(int i=1;i<s.size();i++){
        if(s[i]!=s[i-1]){
            p.push_back({0,i-1});
        }
    }
    if(s[s.size()-1]=='0')p.push_back({0,s.size()-1});
    return p;
}

    kamyak
    4 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    By setting curr = a[i] + b[i], you already ensure that atleast one element is taken which allows empty left and right subarrays. So change curr += left[i - 1] to curr += max(0LL, left[i - 1]. Make similar change to the next line of your code.

    → Reply
